svg.text: BRITISH SIGN LANGUAGE
GRAMMAR — SPATIAL LANGUAGE STRUCTURE
BSL WORD ORDER
BSL: tends toward SVO + topic-comment structures
Topic fronting common: TIME [topic brows] WOMAN GO SHOP
Spatial reference: verb modification replaces many function words
BSL more flexible word order than English (spatial grammar enables this)
BSL NEGATION
BSL negation: headshake simultaneously with signed sentence
NOT sign can also be used; combined NMM + sign = emphatic negation
Incorporated negation: some verbs have negative handshape variant
BSL ASPECT AND TENSE
No morphological tense markers on verbs (like most sign languages)
Time: indicated by timeline in signing space (future = forward, past = back)
Aspect: movement modification (repeated = habitual, slow = prolonged)
DGS GRAMMAR · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
DGS WORD ORDER: SOV BIAS
DGS: tends toward SOV (Subject-Object-Verb), unlike ASL/BSL (SVO)
WOMAN BOOK GIVE = "The woman gives the book" (SOV)
SOV order: matches German spoken grammar! (German is also SOV in subordinate)
DGS VERB AGREEMENT
DGS: rich spatial verb agreement (like ASL and BSL)
Verbs move between loci (locations) to show who does what to whom
More regular agreement morphology than BSL according to some analyses
GRAMMAR COMPARISON
BSL: SVO • DGS: SOV (notable difference between the two)
Both: spatial grammar, verb agreement, NMM, topic-comment
BSL closer to ASL in word order; DGS closer to German spoken word order
Intriguing: sign language grammar partially mirrors surrounding spoken language
PLATE 63c ALT · BSL / DGS · GRAMMAR
